coredata.dc:title: Phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric triple-mode sensor for the detection of acid phosphatase and corresponding inhibitor
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shuai
Chen, Xiao-Bing
Liu, Wen-Ye
Yu, Yong-Liang
Liu, Meng-Xian
coredata.dc:description: Acid phosphatase (ACP) as a clinical diagnostic biomarker for several pathophysiological diseases has aroused widespread interest. Compared to commonly developed single-mode ACP detection technology, the multi-mode detection method with self-validation can provide more reliable results. Herein, we proposed a triple-mode phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric method for ACP detection in combination with CDs@SiO2. HAuCl4 with oxidase-like activity can catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to the blue oxide TMB (TMBox), offering absorption signals and quenching the phosphorescence and fluorescence of CDs@SiO2 based on the internal filtration effect (IFE). ACP can hydrolyze ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) to yield ascorbic acid (AA), thereby reducing TMBox to TMB, triggering solution fading and restoring phosphorescence and fluorescence signals. When the ACP inhibitor malathion is present, the reduction of TMBox is hindered, which successively led to the suppression of CDs@SiO2 phosphorescence and fluorescence signal recovery. According to these principles, triple-mode ACP (LOD = 0.0026 mU mL−1) and malathion detections (LOD = 0.039 μg mL−1) with favorable accuracy and sensitivity are realized. With simplicity, robustness, and versatility, the triple-mode sensor can be extended to the detection of the AAP hydrolase family and the screening of corresponding inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triple-mode detection
Phosphorescence
Fluorescence
Colorimetric
Acid phosphatase
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ACP in real samples
Feasibility of triple-mode sensor for ACP detection
Analytical performance of the triple-mode sensor for ACP detection
Triple-mode sensor for ACP detection in real samples
Triple-mode sensor for ACP inhibitor-malathion detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CDs and CDs@SiO2. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence and phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 (Inset is the photos of CDs@SiO2 under UV light (365 nm) on and off). (C) Full XPS spectra of CDs and CDs@SiO2. (D) FTIR spectra of CDs, SiO2, and CDs@SiO2.

(A) Phosphorescence and fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs@SiO2, and UV–vis absorption spectrum of TMBox. (B) Phosphorescence, (C) Fluorescence, and (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@SiO2 in the existence of different control substrate systems. (a: mere CDs@SiO2, b: CDs@SiO2 + TMB + HAuCl4, c: CDs@SiO2 + TMB + HAuCl4 + AAP + ACP, d: CDs@SiO2 + TMB + HAuCl4 + AAP, e: CDs@SiO2 + TMB + HAuCl4 + ACP; CDs@SiO2 (300 μg mL−1), TMB (1 mM), HAuCl4 (20 μM), AAP (1 mM), ACP (2 mU mL−1). Inset: phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric photos of CDs@SiO2 in the existence of different control substrate systems).

Relative phosphorescence intensity under different concentrations of (A) TMB, (B) AAP, and (C) reaction time (P0 in Fig. 3A refers to the phosphorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2. P0 in Fig. 3B and C refers to the phosphorescence intensity in the existence of both CDs@SiO2, TMB and HAuCl4). (D) Relative phosphorescence, (E) relative fluorescence, and (F) absorbance intensity of the system without ACP, with ACP, and with ACP as well as different interferences (phosphorescence mode: working potential: 750 V, bandwidth EX = 20 nm, bandwidth EM = 20 nm; fluorescence mode: working potential: 500 V, bandwidth EX = 10 nm, bandwidth EM = 10 nm).

(A) Phosphorescence spectra, (C) fluorescence spectra, (E) UV–vis absorption spectra and their corresponding linear relationships between the (B) relative phosphorescence (P/P0), (D) relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0), (F) UV–vis absorbance and the concentrations of ACP (phosphorescence mode: working potential: 750 V, bandwidth EX = 20 nm, bandwidth EM = 20 nm; fluorescence mode: working potential: 500 V, bandwidth EX = 10 nm, bandwidth EM = 10 nm).

(A) Phosphorescence spectra, (C) fluorescence spectra, (E) UV–vis absorption spectra and their corresponding linear relationships between the (B) relative phosphorescence (P/P0), (D) relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0), (F) UV–vis absorbance and malathion concentrations (phosphorescence mode: working potential: 700 V, bandwidth EX = 20 nm, bandwidth EM = 20 nm; fluorescence mode: working potential: 600 V, bandwidth EX = 10 nm, bandwidth EM = 10 nm).
Schematic illustration of triple-mode assay of phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetry for ACP and malathion detection.
The triple-mode analysis results for ACP in serum samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of ACP in 100-fold diluted serum was measured by the Acid Phosphatase Assay Kit.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The triple-mode analysis results for malathion in cabbage samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric triple-mode sensor for the detection of acid phosphatase and corresponding inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors have equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) as a clinical diagnostic biomarker for several pathophysiological diseases has aroused widespread interest. Compared to commonly developed single-mode ACP detection technology, the multi-mode detection method with self-validation can provide more reliable results. Herein, we proposed a triple-mode phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric method for ACP detection in combination with CDs@SiO2. HAuCl4 with oxidase-like activity can catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to the blue oxide TMB (TMBox), offering absorption signals and quenching the phosphorescence and fluorescence of CDs@SiO2 based on the internal filtration effect (IFE). ACP can hydrolyze ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) to yield ascorbic acid (AA), thereby reducing TMBox to TMB, triggering solution fading and restoring phosphorescence and fluorescence signals. When the ACP inhibitor malathion is present, the reduction of TMBox is hindered, which successively led to the suppression of CDs@SiO2 phosphorescence and fluorescence signal recovery. According to these principles, triple-mode ACP (LOD = 0.0026 mU mL−1) and malathion detections (LOD = 0.039 μg mL−1) with favorable accuracy and sensitivity are realized. With simplicity, robustness, and versatility, the triple-mode sensor can be extended to the detection of the AAP hydrolase family and the screening of corresponding inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is a prevalent enzyme that hydrolyses phosphate monoesters to produce inorganic phosphate in acidic conditions with a normal level of 1–11.7 U L−1 in serum [1–4]. Importantly, The abnormal ACP levels in serum are closely related to the development and progression of many diseases, including hyperparathyroidism [5], prostate disease [6], and multiple myeloma [7]. In addition, ACP can also be used as a drug target in drug screening for related diseases [8]. Therefore, it is valuable to construct a precise, economical, and sensitive method for the determination of ACP.

At present, a variety of strategies have been constructed for ACP determination, such as electrochemistry [9], chromatography [10], colorimetry [11,12], and fluorimetry [13–15]. Among these, colorimetric and optical sensors have received extensive attention due to their ease of operation and high sensitivity [11,16–19]. Although colorimetry is simple and fast, the sensitivity is not satisfactory limiting its wide practical application [20]. In recent years, luminescent nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots and gold nanoclusters have been used for quantitative analysis of ACP activity. Although fluorimetry is considered an ideal detection method because of its simplicity of operation and high sensitivity. However, its accuracy and selectivity are susceptible to interference from stray light and background fluorescence in the sample and environment [21]. Multi-mode detection does not involve measuring multiple copies of the same sample using different detection methods. Instead, it entails detecting the same target through multiple signals synchronously triggered by the target. In this scenario, the advantages of various detection methods are integrated, and the measurement results from multiple signals can be compared with each other to eliminate the unreliability of a single method and the inaccuracies caused by the pre-treatment of black box samples, thereby enhancing the detection accuracy [22]. Therefore, the development of detection sensors with multiple output signals can achieve the accurate and highly sensitive detection of ACP.

Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) material, as a new type of luminescent nanomaterial, can emit persistent phosphorescence by slowly releasing trapped carriers under discontinuous excitation, thereby effectively avoiding the influence of autofluorescence and stray light and achieving a high signal-to-noise ratio [23,24]. Now various RTP materials have been developed for biosensing [23,25], information encryption, and anti-counterfeiting [26,27]. Currently, RTP materials are mostly constructed by doping metals into inorganic or organic materials [28]. However, the metals used are mainly noble, resulting in high costs and high cytotoxicity [29]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention for constructing RTP materials because of their abundant precursors, high biocompatibility, and favorable photophysical characteristics [29]. In addition, CDs are easily modified and functionalized and possess wide absorption and emission peaks, making them ideal candidates for the preparation of multimode probes. Therefore, the reasonable design of multi-mode sensors in combination with CD phosphorescent probes is of great value for the construction of ACP detection strategies with broad application prospects.

Here, we develop a triple-mode sensor with phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetry outputs in combination with RTP material-CDs encapsulated in silica (CDs@SiO2) for the accurate and sensitive determination of ACP. As indicated in Scheme 1 , Like an oxidase, HAuCl4 can oxidize the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxide TMB (TMBox), accompanied by the color changing from colorless to blue and showing two wide absorption peaks at 370 and 625 nm [30]. When ACP is present in the system, it can catalyze ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) hydrolysis to produce reducing ascorbic acid (AA), thereby reducing TMBox to TMB. On the one hand, the absorption spectrum of TMBox overlaps with the phosphorescence and fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs@SiO2, thus TMBox can effectively quench the phosphorescence and fluorescence via internal filtration effect (IFE). On the other hand, the reduction of TMBox can decrease the colorimetry signal of the system. According to this strategy, a triple-mode ACP detection method based on phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric was demonstrated. When ACP inhibitor-malathion is present, the activity of ACP is restricted so that it cannot effectively hydrolyze AAP, leading to the inhibition of TMBox reduction and the recovery of phosphorescence and fluorescence signals, thus realizing the determination of malathion. By integrating three detection methods in one sensor, the developed triple-mode sensor can be validated against each other to further improve accuracy and reliability.

1.2 mL of EDA was added to 14.8 mL deionized water, after that 2 mL H3PO4 was introduced to the above EDA aqueous solution with stirring. Then placed in a microwave oven and heated for 130 s at 750 W. Then the products were naturally cooled to room temperature, then 20 mL of deionized water was added and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove precipitates. Then filtered the supernatant with a 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles and neutralized with Na2CO3 (1 M). Collected the above filtrate and dialysis with dialysis bag with an MWCO of 500 Da for 7 days. Finally, pale-yellow CDs powder was received after freeze-drying.

Firstly, 10 mL CDs (0.75 mg mL−1), 2 mL tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), and 0.5 mL NH3·H2O were mixed and stirred at 25 °C for 8 h to obtain the white turbid CDs@SiO2 crude product. Next, the CDs@SiO2 crude product was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large particles. Finally, a clear and transparent CDs@SiO2 solution was obtained after filtering with a 0.22 μm membrane.

20 μL ACP solution with different concentrations, 60 μL AAP (10 mM), and 178 μL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5) were evenly mixed and reacted at 37 °C for 40 min. Then 30 μL TMB (20 mM) and 12 μL HAuCl4 (1 mM) were introduced and shaken for 5 min. Finally, 300 μL of CDs@SiO2 (300 μg mL−1) reserve solution was introduced and shaken thoroughly for 30 s and recorded the signal intensity of the three modes (phosphorescence: 510 nm, fluorescence: 410 nm, and colorimetric: 650 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ACP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Blood sample was collected from healthy volunteers using a procoagulant blood vessel, then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C, and then serum samples were obtained by filtering with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, 20 μL ACP with different concentrations (3, 15, 24 mU mL−1), 60 μL AAP (10 mM), and 6 μL serum samples were mixed thoroughly to prepare spiked samples. The subsequent experiments and signal acquisition were the same as the detection of ACP, except that the additional volume of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5) was reduced to 172 μL.

Firstly, 60 μL of malathion with various concentrations, 30 μL ACP (60 mU mL−1), and 78 μL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5) were mixed and reacted at 37 °C for 15 min. Subsequently, 60 μL AAP was added and reacted for another 40 min. After that, 60 μL TMB (10 mM) and 12 μL HAuCl4 (1 mM) were introduced and shaken for 5 min. Finally, 300 μL CDs@SiO2 (300 μg mL−1) reserve solution was added to the above mixture. After shaking thoroughly for 30 s and recorded the signal intensity of the three modes (phosphorescence: 510 nm, fluorescence: 410 nm, and colorimetric: 650 nm).

Cabbage was bought from Carrefour supermarket (Shenyang, China). 5 g cabbage and 15 mL water were crushed in a wall breaker, then sonicated for 15 min. 400 μL malathion solutions with different concentrations (80, 200, 600 μg mL−1) were spiked to 2 mL of the above mixture and diluted to 20 mL with acetone. After being sonicated for 15 min, the filtrate was collected by 0.22 μm filter membrane. The above filtrate was then heated at 60 °C for 30 min to produce a concentrated solution, then diluted to 4 mL with NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 5) [31]. Finally, the cabbage samples with malathion concentrations of 8, 20, and 60 μg mL−1 were prepared. The solution preparation and signal acquisition were the same as the detection of malathion, except that 60 μL malathion solution was replaced with 60 μL malathion spiked cabbage sample.

CDs@SiO2 with room temperature phosphorescent and fluorescent double emission was synthesized according to the literature with slightly modified [32]. The silica matrix has a significant influence on the luminescent properties of CDs@SiO2. Therefore, to obtain well-dispersed CDs@SiO2 with optimal fluorescence and phosphorescence emission, the amount of silica reagent-TEOS and the reaction time of the hydrolysis and condensation were optimized. As shown in Fig. S1A, increasing the amount of TEOS gradually led to a significant improvement in both fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities of the CDs@SiO2 with a plateau reached at a TEOS addition of 2 mL. However, further increasing the amount of TEOS, the reaction solution turned into a white gel, which may be due to the cross-linking produced by the excess of TEOS. Moreover, extending the reaction time showed a positive correlation with the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities, with a plateau observed after 8 h of reaction (Fig. S1B). Furthermore, to obtain the optimal-performing CDs@SiO2, the quantum yield (QY) of the products was investigated with different amounts of TEOS added (0.1, 0.5, and 2 mL). The results indicated that the highest QY was achieved when 2 mL of TEOS was added (Table S1). Consequently, the CDs@SiO2 samples were prepared using 2 mL of TEOS and an 8-h reaction time. The morphology of the optimized CDs@SiO2 was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs are quasi-spherical with a diameter of approximately 2.3 nm. After TEOS treatment, the diameter of the obtained CDs@SiO2 increased to about 4.0 nm. As a result, CDs are confined to the nanometer space, which limits their vibration and rotation, as well as isolates them from water molecules and dissolved oxygen, leading to water-soluble phosphorescence emission at 510 nm while preserving fluorescence emission at 410 nm (Fig. 1B). This is because confining the CDs in nanospace can effectively suppress their vibration and rotation, thus reducing the proportion of non-radiative transitions. In addition, the ground state of oxygen in an aqueous solution is in a triplet state and readily receives electrons from the triplet state of the CDs thus becoming singlet oxygen to effectively burst the phosphorescence of CDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy tests of CDs and CDs@SiO2 were carried out to investigate their structure and the form of CDs encapsulated in silicon shells. Compared with the full XPS spectrum of CDs, CDs@SiO2 shows a distinct Si peak (Fig. 1C). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2) demonstrates C–Si (284.1 eV), C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C–N/C–O (285.7 eV), and CO (288.3 eV) bonds [33–36]. In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum, the peaks at 401.9 and 399.8 eV are raised from N–H and C–N bonds [37–39]. While those in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (532.9 and 532.4 eV) belong to C–O/Si–O [35,40] and P–O [41,42] bonds. In addition, from the high-resolution Si 2p spectrum, it is observed that C–Si (103.0 eV) bond, Si–O–C (103.6 eV) bond, and Si-Ox (104.2 eV) bond are present on the surface of CDs@SiO2 [35,43,44]. The fact that the surface of CD is rich in hydroxyl groups provides abundant sites for hydrolysis and condensation with TEOS (Fig. 1D, orange line). After reacting with TEOS, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1D) of CDs@SiO2 displays the significant Si–OH (3454 cm−1) and Si–O–Si (1089 cm−1), and C–Si (890 cm−1) bonds [45]. To better demonstrate the application potential of the CDs@SiO2, their photostability and time stability were explored. The results show that the phosphorescence and fluorescence intensities of CDs@SiO2 are almost unchanged after 30 min of continuous photoexcitation and that the changes in fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity are negligible after 15 days of storage at room temperature (25 °C), indicating their favorable photostability and time stability (Figs. S3A–D). In addition, the CDs@SiO2 also exhibit favorable ionic strength and pH stability (Figs. S3E–H). The above results confirm the successful synthesis of CDs@SiO2 with aqueous phase fluorescence and phosphorescence dual emission as well as favorable stability by covalent bonding of CDs encapsulated in silica.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of triple-mode sensor for ACP detection
ce:section.ce:para: HAuCl4 with oxidase-like activity can oxidize TMB to turn from colorless to blue TMBox [30]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TMBox overlaps with the phosphorescence and fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 2 A), indicating that TMBox would quench both of these luminescent signals of the CDs@SiO2 through the IFE [46]. TMBox can be reduced to TMB by AA produced by ACP-catalyzed AAP hydrolysis, leading to a visible color change and recovery of fluorescence and phosphorescence signals.

The feasibility of the triple-mode sensor was investigated by incubating several control substrate systems. According to Fig. 2B–D, the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity at 410 nm and 510 nm were significantly reduced after the addition of TMB and HAuCl4, while the absorbance at 650 nm was significantly enhanced (lines a and b). From the inset in Fig. 2D, it's observed that the solution turns blue after adding TMB and HAuCl4, suggesting that TMB has been oxidized by HAuCl4 into blue TMBox, and the produced TMBox effectively busted the phosphorescence and fluorescence of CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 2B and C). When AAP and ACP were simultaneously added to the CDs@SiO2 + TMB + HAuCl4 system, the intensities of fluorescence at 410 nm and phosphorescence at 510 nm were obviously recovered, and the blue color of the solution was weakened, accompanied by the weakening of the absorbance at 650 nm (lines b and c), indicating that the hydrolysate-AA of AAP successfully reduced the blue TMBox to colorless TMB, and thence effectively reducing the quenching effect of the two luminescent signals of CDs@SiO2. In addition, when AAP or ACP was added alone, the intensities of the three modes were almost unchanged, indicating that the sensor can accurately quantify enzyme activity without interference from the enzyme and substrate itself (lines b, d, and e).

To verify the quenching mechanism of the phosphorescence and fluorescence signals of CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2 lifetime tests before and after adding TMBox were carried out. It's reported that the difference between dynamic quenching and static quenching is whether there are changes in the excited state lifetime. The results show that the average phosphorescence and fluorescence lifetime of CDs@SiO2 changes negligibly after adding TMBox (Fig. S4). In addition, combined with the overlapping among the absorption spectrum of TMBox and excitation spectra of CDs@SiO2, it is reasonable to attribute the luminescence quenching to the static quenching caused by IFE. In addition, the phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric signal intensities are related to AA concentrations (0–10 μM) (Fig. S5), suggesting an indirect correlation with the ACP concentration. Therefore, the constructed sensing platform is expected to perform a triple-mode of phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric signals for ACP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the triple-mode sensor for ACP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire high ACP sensing performance, the concentration of TMB and AAP as well as reaction time were optimized by taking the phosphorescence mode as an example. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the decline of the phosphorescence up to 84% was observed after the addition of TMB and HAuCl4 and plateaued at a TMB concentration of 1 mM. In addition, the optimal phosphorescence signal recovery effect can be obtained at a substrate-AAP concentration of 1 mM (Fig. 3B). It is evident from Fig. 3C that the phosphorescent recovery efficiency continued to increase with the prolongation of the reaction time and appeared to plateau by 40 min. Therefore, 1 mM TMB, 1 mM AAP, and 40 min reaction time were selected for the following experiments.

Following optimization experiments, each method against various interferences was investigated to determine its selectivity. The effects of metal ions (Na+, K+, and Ca2+) and several other substances (glucose, GOx, pepsase, ALP, AchE, AA, and GSH) commonly found in biological systems were examined. As shown in Fig. 3D–F, when the target and these potential interferences coexist in the system, the triple-mode signals (P/P0, F/F0, and A/A0) show no significant changes, indicating its favorable selectivity and practical application potential.

The performance of the triple-mode sensor in the detection of ACP was explored under the optimal reaction conditions to evaluate its sensitivity and accuracy. The yield of AA increases with the concentration of ACP and subsequently triggers a differential reduction reaction of TMBox, resulting in a weakening of the colorimetric signal and IFE, and eventually a recovery of phosphorescence and fluorescence signals. As displayed in phosphorescence mode, the relative phosphorescence intensity at 510 nm increases linearly when the ACP concentrations were varied from 0.005 to 2 mU mL−1, and the calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.0026 mU mL−1 (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4 A and B). As to fluorescence mode, the linear response concentration is 0.02–1.5 mU mL−1, and the LOD is 0.013 mU mL−1 (Fig. 4C and D). While those of colorimetric mode are 0.05–1.5 mU mL−1 and 0.044 mU mL−1, respectively (Fig. 4E and F). Compared with the reported sensors, the triple-mode methodology possesses universal sensitivity toward ACP detection (Table S2) and in accordance with expectation, the phosphorescence mode exhibits the widest linear range and lowest LOD.

Because of the low background interference of the phosphorescent assay, the detection of ACP in a serum matrix was carried out to demonstrate the advantages of phosphorescent mode detection over fluorescence. The signal recovery rate means the ratio of the signal of the substrate system in the existence of ACP to the initial signal of the substrate system (CDs@SiO2 + TMB + HAuCl4 + AAP). The signal recovery rates of both modes increased along with the increasing ACP concentration in each measurement solution (Fig. S6A). Meanwhile, it is obvious that at the same ACP concentration, the signal recovery rate for phosphorescence mode is consistently higher than that for fluorescence mode, confirming that phosphorescence mode possesses a higher sensitivity. To uncover the reason for the higher sensitivity of the phosphorescent mode, the background signal intensities of phosphorescence and fluorescence in HAc-NaAc buffer and serum matrix were carried out. It was found that the fluorescent background signals intensity of buffer and serum matrix were 30 and 66 a. u., respectively, while that of phosphorescence mode remained as low as in HAc-NaAc buffer and were almost zero. (HAc-NaAc buffer: 0.55; serum matrix: 0.66) (Fig. S6B). The strong autofluorescence signals in the serum matrix can be attributed to the luminescent biomolecules, such as proteins in the serum. Since the phosphorescent mode detection was conducted without real-time excitation, the signal interference was avoided and a sensitive detection was achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triple-mode sensor for ACP detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery tests were carried out on human serum samples (1%) with three different spiking concentrations to assess the application potential of the ACP sensor. The inherent concentration of ACP in the serum was determined using an Acid Phosphatase Assay Kit, and the measured result was 6.37 mU mL−1, which is consistent with the reported normal levels of ACP in human serum [2–5]. In addition, the spiked recoveries of the three modes in the sensor were all in the range of 96%–105% at three concentrations, confirming the stable performance and high accuracy of the triple-mode sensor (Table 1 ). The Relative Standard Deviation (RSD), which represents the ratio of variability in results to the mean value, is commonly used to assess the differences between results and evaluate the reproducibility and repeatability of a method. The results demonstrate that this method exhibits low RSD values (<4.78%) in ACP detection in serum, indicating its high reproducibility and repeatability. T-test is a commonly used significance evaluation method, which is often used to compare whether there is a significant difference between the means of two samples. Therefore, T-test was conducted to investigate whether there was a significant difference between the found value and the standard added value under the three modes, to evaluate the accuracy of the sensor. The results show that when the confidence level is 99%, the calculated T values between the found value of the three modes and the standard values are all smaller than t0.01,2 = 9.92, suggesting that there was no significant difference in the found values of the spiked samples among phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetry modes (Fig. S7), confirming the great potential of the triple-mode sensor in accurate and robust detection of ACP in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triple-mode sensor for ACP inhibitor-malathion detection
ce:section.ce:para: There are many inhibitors such as sodium vanadate (Na3VO4), sodium fluoride (NaF), methyl parathion (PM), malathion, etc., which can inhibit the enzyme activity of ACP [31,47,48]. Therefore, we speculated that based on the concentration dependent inhibition efficiency of these inhibitors on ACP, the application scope of the triple-mode sensor could be extended to the detection of corresponding inhibitors. Among them, malathion is also a highly effective insecticide, widely used for pest control in rice, wheat, vegetables, fruit trees, etc. Despite its great economic benefits, it is a Class 2 A carcinogen, causing severe threats to human health, including causing numbness, difficulty breathing, tremors, motor incoordination, vision loss, and other symptoms. Therefore, malathion was used as a model to investigate the applicability of this system in the detection of ACP inhibitors.

As the concentration of malathion increases, the catalytic activity of ACP is gradually inhibited, which triggers a decrease in AA production and TMBox reduction, ultimately leading to the quenching of phosphorescence and fluorescence signals (Fig. 5 ). As shown in the phosphorescence mode (Fig. 5A and B), the relative phosphorescence intensity values (P/P0) decreased linearly with the malathion concentration obey the equation of y = −0.0178x + 0.998, on a scale of 0.05–20 μg mL−1 with a LOD of 0.039 μg mL−1. Not surprisingly, the linear ranges of the fluorescence (0.08−20 μg mL−1) and colorimetric (0.08−20 μg mL−1) modes are somewhat shortened compared to the phosphorescence mode and show higher LODs of 0.059 μg mL−1 and 0.065 μg mL−1, respectively (Fig. 5C–F). In addition, compared with the reported sensors, the triple-mode platform possesses a comparable linear range and sensitivity for malathion detection (Table S2).

Favorable selectivity is an important prerequisite for realizing practical application value. Therefore, the selectivity of the constructed triple-mode sensor in the determination of malathion (15 μg mL−1) against interferences was assessed to evaluate its application potential. As shown in Fig. S8, when common residual metal ions (K+, Na+, and Fe2+) and anions (PO4 3-, CO3 2-, HCO3 −, and SO4 2-) in fruits and vegetables coexist with malathion, the three-mode response signal does not change significantly compared to that in the presence of malathion alone, indicating that these potentially interfering substances have negligible influence on the response of the sensor to malathion, indicating the great potential of the sensor for malathion determination in real samples.

Finally, the usefulness of the sensor was confirmed by detecting malathion in an actual sample matrix. Since malathion can remain in fruits and vegetables, cabbage was selected as the actual sample for the adding recovery experiment. Three real samples of cabbage with concentrations of 0.8, 2, and 6 μg mL−1 of malathion were constructed by the standard addition method, and then the sensor was used to test the recovery rate to examine its accuracy. Table 2 shows that adding standard recoveries of malathion in cabbage range from 88% to 106% with RSD less than 5.63%, and when the confidence level is set at 99%, the T-test test results in Fig. S9 show that there is no significant difference between the found values under the three modes and the standard added value, confirming the satisfactory accuracy and robustness of the sensor in malathion determination in real samples.

In summary, a sensor with triple-mode signal output is constructed for accurate and robust detection of ACP and malathion by rationally integrating phosphorescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric analysis. The sensor is based on the ACP-triggered TMBox reduction reaction, inhibition of ACP activity by malathion, and their potential effects on CDa@SiO2 luminescence and system absorbance. With this principle, high performance triple-mode detections of ACP and malathion have been demonstrated, and its reliability and robustness in real samples are verified. The ACP could be quantified in the range of 0.005–2 mU mL−1 with a LOD of 0.0026 mU mL−1. And that of malathion is from 0.05 to 20 μg mL−1 with a low LOD down to 0.039 μg mL−1. Given its facile fabrication and satisfactory performance, the sensor is expected to be of great promise in the detection of the AAP hydrolase family and the screening of corresponding inhibitors.

Shuai Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiao-Bing Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wen-Ye Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Yong-Liang Yu: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Meng-Xian Liu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922402, 21874017 and 21727811), Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021-MS-099) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2205004 and N2005027). Special thanks to the instrumental analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University (China).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.341612.
